1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - People, the Employees"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1242-1262"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**PEOPLE, THE EMPLOYEES**
     9→
    10→**The role of people in a corporate**  
    11→As we know and say, people are the most important assets of an organisation. An organisation does not comprise of a name and a huge building made of bricks and mortar. It’s the people who make the name and fame of an organisation; it’s the people who execute the business to achieve its objectives and build reputation for the organization. 
    12→
    13→Other assets are also important. But unlike the yesteryears, they are comparatively easier to build. For example, an organisation today, even if it’s size is small, has multiple sources to obtain finance, be it banks or other financial institutions, at low interest rates. With such finance, it can purchase good quality equipment and other infrastructure, software and hardware to conduct its business. With money, you can easily invest good quality assets for your business.
    14→
    15→But what about good quality professionals? Unfortunately, they don’t come with an ISO stamp as a product or service does. Yes, they do come with academic degrees behind them, but it has been proven time and again that the best academic professionals of reputed universities or colleges are not necessarily the best experienced and talented people to deliver in organisations. Academic degrees do provide the necessary criteria to appear for an interview and are indicative of supposedly good performance, but the HR Head has to be very cautious to select a professional who can practically deliver the best in the industry.
    16→
    17→Choosing the right candidate today is difficult, more so in a populous country with millions having the required academic degrees and experience. But today you need to choose the best and leave out even the good ones. Here is why it’s so.
    18→
    19→For example, in a tender driven business, you need to become L1 with a technical and a financial responsive bid. Even if you have submitted a very good bid but are L2 or higher by a thin margin, it has no meaning except for condolences from your boss for good work. Because the L1 presumably wins the contract and once you execute the contract, not only the proceeds contribute to the finances of the organisation, but also enable the organisation to realise the profits and pay your salaries, increment, bonus, bear the overheads and grow the organization. The L2 bidder has no significance except for a consolation that its price was competitive. Therefore you need the best people in the industry, to become L1 as in the above example.
    20→
    21→Today bad people are flocking all around. Good people are abundant but the best are scarce. So the HR has a tough job to find the best which is required to keep the organization afloat amidst stiff competition and continuous changing environment with each passage of time.
    22→
    23→Also it may be noted that organisations who have long term plans in a particular business domain needs to recruit the best though their salary demand may be higher. Investment in the best of people will result in good financials in the long run. In Bengali, there is a well-known phrase. It goes like this: “Sastar teen obostha” which means “buying anything cheap will lead to three disasters”. 
    24→
    25→It is the best people in the industry who carves a niche for their organisation and creates such product and services which eventually become their USP. For example, look at the GE engines used by aircraft manufacturers. They have almost become indispensable. It is GE’s USP. 
    26→
    27→Hire the best, use the best and be the best in the industry. If you recruit average people, don’t expect anything better than average business results.
    28→

1→---
     2→title: "THE ‘TRIPLE S’ CHARACTER"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1263-1296"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE ‘TRIPLE S’ CHARACTER**
     9→
    10→In a corporate, everyone desires a good pay, recognition, position and importance. There are two methods to achieve this. 
    11→
    12→One, be a blind follower of your boss and seniors who matters for achieving your desires. Just follow as advised without confronting and ignoring what is right or wrong. Simply act as they wish. This is the SSS character.
    13→
    14→1st S \- Spineless, having no personality to revert what is right or wrong  
    15→2nd S \- Speechless \- afraid to table your point of view  
    16→3rd S \- Sightless \- having no vision to understand the consequences of your silence.
    17→
    18→Though you can achieve your desires in this “SSS” way, such achievement comes with guilt and shame which may disturb your sleep at times\!
    19→
    20→Two, rise and achieve your desires by proving your performance and talent. The talented never follow the “SSS” way to rise up the corporate ladder. They will strive to perform the best always, whatsoever challenges and hardships they might have to face, sometimes struggling even against their bosses. 
    21→
    22→Why are “SSS” characters so common? Do you think they are fools? Not at all. I call them “character less non-performers but achievers”. With such ‘SSS’ attributes, they hope that they will receive favours unethically from their bosses when it comes to appraisal for promotion, performance bonus and increment. They don’t care about their “SSS” character; they know their “non-performance”, hence resort to such behaviors just for receiving favours. I was astonished to observe some talent to resort to SSS way. Initially I was puzzled as to why they are taking up this unethical route for growth when they are good performers themselves. But later I discovered that these performers wished to get the best increment and bonus and a promotion to such a higher extent that they actually do not deserve, hence their SSS character.    
    23→
    24→Once, an employee leaked personal information about his boss to all his colleagues. The boss had kept this information a secret. The boss came to know about this. The HR Head also knew about it. Now the boss wanted to sack the employee who divulged the secret. The employee was a reasonably good performer but had issues with his attendance in the organization. Taking advantage of this, the boss ganged up with the HR Head and the Project Head trying to find out a way to sack the employee. The boss asked the HR Head to give a bad report on his attendance and asked the Project Head to give a bad report on his performance. Upon getting these bad reports, the boss appealed to the CHRO to sack the employee and eventually was successful in doing that.   
    25→The employee had the guts and complained to the Board and the Board understood that the employee was victimised. The HR Head and the Project Head were both reprimanded. The employee was again reinstated. 
    26→
    27→In the above case, both the HR Head and the Project Head demonstrated their ‘SSS’ character following the advice of their boss instead of looking into the merits of the case. They were spineless because they lacked courage to protest on the wrong doing of the boss. They were speechless because they were afraid even to talk. And they were sightless because they lacked vision about the possible consequences of what actually happened.
    28→
    29→Demonstrate your knowledge, experience, talent, capability and determination so that the leaders take note of it. Rise up the corporate ladder on your strength. At times, you may face conflicting situations with your seniors, but be it so. Try to handle such events with strategy and politeness. Report to your seniors and HR Head the resistance from your boss for your performance and your growth in the organization. 
    30→
    31→One thing is crystal clear and be sure about it. If you have the talent, no one can stop you from going to the top; however, you may face some stiff hurdles occasionally from jealous people trying to stop your growth. These hurdles may sometimes slow your growth, but eventually you will gain in the long run by your sheer talent and determination.
    32→
    33→Never assume the 'SSS' character.
    34→
    35→**The salient points are:**
    36→
    37→1. Never be spineless, speechless and sightless (the SSS character)  
    38→2. Rise up to the occasion to report unethical doings of leaders  
    39→3. Demonstrate your talent and performance to rise up the corporate ladder  
    40→4. Don’t get bogged down by some people trying to hamper your growth; negotiate your way with strategy and talent, you will be bound to succeed.
    41→

1→---
     2→title: "THE INCAPABLE MANAGERS ON TOP"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1297-1320"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE INCAPABLE MANAGERS ON TOP**
     9→
    10→Have you seen a wheel knob in a 2-door refrigerator in the earlier models? You will find that if you turn the wheel at one extreme end then the freezer compartment temperature will be highest and the refrigerator compartment temperature will be lowest. Similarly, if you turn the wheel at the other extreme end then the freezer compartment temperature will be lowest and the refrigerator compartment temperature will be highest. And if you turn the wheel in between then both the compartment temperatures will be moderate.
    11→
    12→Similar is the concept of positions in an organization like the temperature wheel of a 2-door refrigerator. The lowest positions, say in the Junior Management, will call for hard core technical work but almost none of core managerial work. The highest positions on the other hand, say in the Top Management, will call for hard core managerial work but very less of technical work. And in the Middle Management, you will have a mix of technical and managerial work.
    13→
    14→Technical work relates to the technicalities of a project like the casting of foundations, erecting a super structure, installing lifts, painting and wooden works, etc. while managerial work will involve managing people, interaction with the client, realization of payments from client, building customer-client relationship, attending top level meetings, etc. 
    15→
    16→However, I find it astonishing that organisations do post people at top positions (not the Design or Engineering Heads) who are deeply involved with the technical work. Hence the managerial aspects that he is supposed to handle take a backseat. 
    17→
    18→Once I noticed a Project Director deeply involved in the designs of structures in the project with the designers. While his job was to oversee the progress of the project, his main duty was to manage people at site, interface with the site and head office, process payments from the client, find whether the client is fulfilling its obligations under the contract, involve in claims management, find out future business opportunities, etc. but instead of all these he spent lot of time in discussing the nitty-gritties of designs with the Design Head.
    19→
    20→I am not here to demine any kind of work, technical or managerial. But I wanted to stress on the job profiles usually allotted to different levels in an organization. It is clear that you are supposed to perform more of managerial work in a senior position and not get involved into technical work which would in any case be handled by the Middle and Junior Management. 
    21→
    22→As another example, once a Project Director was assigned to a 220 kV electric substation being constructed in another state. The Project Director was very interested in the technical specifications of the various products involved in the project, viz. transformers, isolators, etc. He was also keenly involved in the construction methodologies and site engineering for the project but ignored the financial aspects of the project like the profitability analysis from time to time, the Cash Flow involved, the time management required for completion, the overhead costs he employed at site and office and had no time to explore possible future business with the client.
    23→
    24→This is not what a Project Director’s profile should be. Such profiles do not fit for senior leadership roles. There is nothing wrong if someone is interested in the designs and engineering of a project and little bit of management at site, but then such individuals should be kept at Middle Management levels. At senior Management levels, you have to be involved much more with the business and financials of the organization than the technical issues involved in the project. For the Top Management, other than the Designs and Engineering personnel, the wheel of the refrigerator must be at one extreme, not at the middle or wrongly at the other extreme.  
    25→     
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Top Management must be involved most with management and least with technical issues; Middle management must be involved with a more or less equal mix of managerial and technical works; Junior Management must be involved most with technical works and least with core managerial works.  
    29→2. Above is so, because as illustrated earlier, Top management directs, Middle management facilitates and Junior management implements  
    30→3. People not adhering to above principles should be repositioned by HR or the Top management.
    31→

1→---
     2→title: "DEPARTMENTAL HEADS AT LOW POSITIONS"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1321-1340"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : DEPARTMENTAL HEADS AT LOW POSITIONS**
     9→
    10→You have seen the flow of levels in an organization chart, from the BOD to Managing Director to CXOs to SBU Heads to BU Heads & Departmental Heads.
    11→
    12→Above are basically functional positions. Below the MD and CXOs, i.e. the SBU Heads, BU Heads and Departmental Heads have managerial designations such as the Executive Vice President, Vice President, Asst. Vice President, Sr. General Manager, General Manager, Asst. General Manager, Deputy General Manager, Senior Manager, Manager, Asst. Manager, Sr. Officer, Officer, etc.
    13→
    14→If a Departmental Head is holding a junior designation, say, a Manager, who normally reports to the SBU Head who is a Vice President, then there is every chance that the SBU Head can misuse his powers and threaten or convince the Manager for doing something which is unethical. If Departmental Heads assume low designations, then such Departmental Heads must refer to their CHRO or to their Functional Heads in the Head Office in case SBU Head or the BU Head proposes something abnormal or unethical. The point I am making is that the positions between the SBU Head, BU Head and Departmental Heads should not be widely different, otherwise, there may be a chance of the seniors exploiting the Departmental Heads who are much junior to him. This happens because Departmental Heads assuming junior positions are afraid to be victimised later on, particularly on annual appraisals.
    15→
    16→In one example, a senior employee in the rank of a GM got promoted to VP and invited many senior colleagues including his boss for a treat in a reputed restaurant. When the treat was over, his boss advised the HR Head, who was the Departmental Head but only a Senior Manager by designation, to consider the restaurant bill as official (ie. to be considered as a treat to a client). Though unethical, the HR Head had to follow the instruction and made the bill official. After reimbursement, the money was given back to the VP, therefore, having paid nothing. In this case, the boss wanted to please the GM, now a VP, (reverse oiling) to keep him in the good books with an expectation that all critical client problems, which were prevailing at that point of time, would be handled by him without disturbing the boss.
    17→
    18→In another case, a boss disliked an employee for personal reasons. He asked the HR Departmental Head, a Manager this time, and the BU Head, a Dy. GM by designation, to find out discredits of the employee. The HR Head found out that the employee was not attending office on time on many days. The Dy. GM found that some areas of project were not handled by the employee well, which of course was the case with many other employees. Thereafter, the boss made a case to the CHRO proposing removal of the employee from the organization. Based on the reports of the HR Head and the BU Head, the employee was sacked. In this case, the HR Head and the BU Head did not have the guts to say anything on the wrong doings of the boss. This happens when Departmental Heads assume much junior designations with respect to his senior bosses sitting on very high designations.
    19→
    20→Therefore the lesson is HR Head must ensure that Departmental Heads too have higher designations, comparable to his seniors/bosses, otherwise they may be exploited at times as the cases above illustrate. 
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. Position Departmental Heads comparable to his senior bosses  
    25→2. Departmental Heads, if holding junior positions, may be exploited or forced to agree to whimsical and unethical ways of the bosses  
    26→3. Departmental Heads, if at all holding junior positions and cannot confront  their bosses in case of wrong doings, may bring up the matter to their CHRO or to their Functional Heads in Head Office
    27→

1→---
     2→title: "TIME MISMANAGEMENT"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1341-1375"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : TIME MISMANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→An experienced professional does not necessarily mean he is time conscious and meticulous in planning his time. I have seen many seniors rushing to work as soon as they reach office. This demonstrates their sincerity to work. But unfortunately they may do things in the beginning which are not urgent or insignificant. 
    11→
    12→I always tell people not to work for the first 30 minutes in your office, rather plan to prioritise and sequence your various works that you have in hand. There could be four kinds of jobs.
    13→
    14→1. Urgent & significant  
    15→2. Urgent but not significant  
    16→3. Not urgent but significant  
    17→4. Not urgent and not significant
    18→
    19→Urgent are those which need immediate attention. Significant are those which have considerable impacts, technically or financially, for the overall results.
    20→
    21→Even within urgent works, you need to prioritise depending on the time required to go through the case or on the significant impact of the case. If any urgent work requires a few minutes of introspection, it is better to complete and dispose them off first. In this way, the number of urgent works left can be reduced drastically. In case of non-urgent works, those with higher impacts may be taken up first.
    22→
    23→Examples of these four kinds of jobs:
    24→
    25→1. (a) Urgent and significant and requiring less time to dispose: eg. Payment of Rs 10 Crores to a vendor urgently so that the equipment can be installed on time and the project completed by deadline in order to avoid Consequential Damages  
    26→   (b) Urgent and significant but requiring more time to dispose: eg. Cash Flow of a newly acquired project to be submitted for the Board for immediate approval   
    27→     
    28→2. Urgent but not significant: eg. Approval for flight travel of an employee the next day so that the Administration can book his flight ticket
    29→
    30→3. Not urgent but significant – eg. Approval of monthly overhead costs of a company: The company may have incurred a significant cost as its overheads due to office maintenance, repair works, travel costs of employees, car and fuel, etc. but this approval is not so urgent. It can even wait for a few days for approval because the cost may be significant but the delay will not hamper the business.  
    31→     
    32→4. Not urgent and not significant: eg. Approval of purchase of snacks on a daily basis for employees and clients over the week or month – Such a case is neither urgent nor it has any significant cost impact. I remember when I was the SBU Head, every day at 5 pm, the office staff used to come and sign off the daily purchase cost of snacks. Invariably my ongoing meetings used to get disturbed and our focus on the subject distracted. Thereafter I asked the office staff to come with his daily bills only once a week every Friday at 5 pm. This mundane work streamlined normal operations.
    33→
    34→Prioritise all jobs in hand in the sequence as shown or as you find relevant. The purpose is to complete the urgent works first and dispose them off as many as possible within a short period of time. We are all human beings and the Almighty have given each of us only two hands and one head to work\! We cannot multitask always with equal sincerity and quality. So we need to complete one work after another. Therefore serious planning is required to sequence the jobs in the above order. It is often said once the plan is excellent and in place, half the job is done.
    35→
    36→**The salient points are:**
    37→
    38→1. Spend 30 minutes every day to list and plan your sequence of jobs  before you actually start working  
    39→2. Priotorise your works in order; urgent works first and the others later  
    40→3. Proper planning to pritorising your works is half the job done  
    41→4. Less important jobs can be delegated to speed up your jobs
    42→

1→---
     2→title: "STRESSED ENVIORONMENT"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1376-1406"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : STRESSED ENVIORONMENT**
     9→
    10→Today, every individual lives with stress, big or small. However, if the stress levels are lesser, the better it is for the health of an individual. You must have seen in newspapers, magazines and the social media about sudden heart attacks and cardiac arrests happening across young people too. I understand that many of them are related to stress and if not, at least triggered by stress.
    11→
    12→Stress can be of different types; emotional stress (e.g. Death of a near and dear one, break up of a lover, rude behavior of a close friend, etc), societal stress (e.g. Divorce, loss of job, etc), financial stress (e.g. Lack of adequate finance to run livelihood), corporate stress (e.g. stress imposed by his boss in office), mental stress (e.g. fear of losing job, being accused by someone), physical stress (e.g. induced through lot of physical work which the body is unable to bear).  In a 24 hour day, we spend 10 to 12 hours in office itself.  So corporate stress combined with other stresses can create havoc to an individual.
    13→
    14→Here’s an example how a CBO induced huge stress to a SBU Head who was given a target of Rs 5000 Crores order book for a year in his business domain. With market intelligence and acquaintances across competitors and preview to the latest Balance Sheets published by different companies, the SBU Head tried to figure out the total targets given by each of the competitor companies by their Top Management. It was a whopping Rs 40,000 Crores per year. But the industry had a Rs 20,000 Crore business per annum. Imagine marketing of all companies fighting for Rs 40,000 Crores order book in a Rs 20,000 Crore industry\! Imagine the plight and stress of all the Marketing people.
    15→
    16→What happens if the targets are practically unachievable? I have already discussed such SMURT targets in one of the earlier chapters. The SBU Heads know that competitors are no fools. Some will get more and some will get fewer orders, but the summation of all orders of all companies put together cannot exceed Rs 20,000 Crores as is available in the industry. The CBO estimates that say, Rs 3000 Crore could have been an achievable target given to the SBU Head based on the size of the industry and the competitor’s past performance. However he does not spell out this ‘achievable’ target. Instead, the CBO demands the SBU Head for a Rs 5000 Crore annual target of order book. The SBU Head and its team thereafter slogs out day and night to achieve such “unachievable” targets destroying mental peace, having untimely meals, ignoring health, ignoring parents, children, relatives and friends. Eventually the team may be successful to obtain Rs 3500 Crore order against the Rs 5000 Crore target. Remember, the internal secret target of the CBO was Rs 3000 Crore. By pressurizing them with a Rs 5000 Crore target, the SBU team could obtain Rs 500 Crore order more for the company with respect to the internal secret target. The CBO is happy and so are his bosses.
    17→
    18→The CBO might have won the heart of his boss and get a promotion or good increment. Promotions may have happened even down below; but what about your deteriorated health that need the attention of the doctors or the hospital. The CBO will say it is SBU Head and its team’s prerogative in which way they should work. The CBO shrugs his shoulder off and takes no responsibility for the ill health of his employees down below. Some may die due to heart attack or cardiac arrests as I mentioned before being unable to take the stress of achieving the unachievable targets. 
    19→
    20→The most interesting thing is the company takes care only to the extent of providing the medical insurance for you, but what about the permanent damage caused to your body and the precautions you need to take for the rest of your life. The company is scot free. You suffer for your entire life only to get a higher increment and promotion which you shall repent forever.
    21→
    22→Money is very important to one’s life. Promotions, bonus and increments are too. No one can dispute this. But does it make sense if they are at the cost of your permanent health damage\! We all run after more money disregarding our health, body, mind and soul and also our parents, family and friends.
    23→
    24→What are the solutions? The Head and the subordinates must have a detailed discussion to fix a difficult but reasonably achievable target so that the job called for is “SMART” and not “SMURT”. Trust must exist between the Heads and his subordinates. Secondly, the Heads and subordinates should periodically review the performance of the company as well as its competitors with respect to the business domain and modify or tweak the targets if required depending on how the competition and the industry change. The Heads must keep the targets difficult but they must be ‘achievable’. In this way, there will be trust between the Management and down below and the management will display humanity by not unnecessarily stressing them and their families. The employees still have to work hard to achieve difficult but achievable targets but can probably keep a check on their mental and physical health. We are all humans, and we need to be treated with humanity even in the corporates, not treated like the deers who have to run at their life’s best speed every day to save themselves from being eaten by the tigers.
    25→
    26→In many instances, the site manager is overloaded and stressed with the job for completion of a project at the end of the scheduled completion period. Many projects, for various reasons, do not progress as desired or envisaged. Therefore, before the scheduled completion period, there is a huge overload to complete the project on time; otherwise there may be severe penalties that the organization has to face. In such a case, the site manager and his team slog out day and night ignoring their health and families because of the pressure and dictate from the top. Sometimes these stress levels cross beyond a limit that an individual can tolerate and fatal health issues may crop up.
    27→
    28→Many countries have taken up this grave issue. For example, ‘The right to ignore’ law in Australia and some European countries ensure that duties being allotted by bosses outside office hours may be ignored. The bosses have right to allot work as and when they desire but only during office hours. The law has been enacted so that employees can pursue a work-life balance and also enable ‘me time’ to themselves. Corporates must learn that the psychological health too is of prime importance to individuals.
    29→
    30→There is a limit to which an individual can be stressed. A good quality rubber band can keep objects in place tightly, but if stretched beyond limits, it will snap and you will lose the opportunity to tie the objects. Similarly, if you stress a good performer beyond limit, the body, mind and soul will fail and the organization will lose a good talent.
    31→
    32→**The salient points are:**
    33→
    34→1. As a good leader, provide SMART targets, not SMURT ones  
    35→2. Understand competition, opportunities in the industry and then provide targets to your employees which shall be difficult yet possible to achieve  
    36→3. Give importance to your employee’s health, ensure their work-life balance and provide ‘me time’ to all individuals. They may be employees with stiff targets, but at the same time, they have the right to lead dignified lives.  
    37→4. Bad health of good performers will eventually be a loss to the organization.
    38→

1→---
     2→title: "THE APCS MENACE"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2
     5→original_lines: "1407-1457"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE APCS MENACE**
     9→
    10→APCS is a nomenclature I have coined. You will not get it in the dictionary. At the end of the chapter, I am sure you will realise its importance.
    11→
    12→Today, we live in a world of adulteration (A), pollution (P), corruption (C), and  Stress (S), ie the APCS environment. This is a very big health and mental hazard. While the ‘A’ and ‘P’ destroy mental health, ‘C’ and ‘S’ destroy mental health. 
    13→
    14→Food adulteration is not new in many countries including India. We see it often in different media. I have heard that brinjals are wax polished to shine beautifully, pointed gourds are dipped in copper sulphate solution to give a fresh green look, watermelons are injected with red colour dyes to give inside a pretty good reddish look, bottle gourds are injected to become large in size very fast, bananas are riped with carbide, so on and so forth.
    15→
    16→I also hear that in groceries, brick powder is mixed in red chili powder, white sand is mixed in white salt, small iron chips are mixed in similarly looking small tea leaves, chemicals added to make artificial paneer, so on and so forth. Then we understand that polished dal is bad, hot pressed oil is not good for health, packaged food is very salty and contains trans fats, processed food is laden with saturated fat, so on and so forth.
    17→
    18→We also come across the rising importance of buying organic vegetables, organic foods, organic groceries, cold pressed oil, food without preservatives, etc. But these products are very costly.      7→
     8→**CHAPTER : GROWING  BY AGE, NOT BY KNOWLEDGE**
     9→
    10→Everyone wants to be a top leader someday. Everyone wishes to grow up the corporate ladder and reach to the top. Isn't it? So, are you also counting the number of years of experience gathered after your college? 
    11→
    12→Please don’t count the years. Today, corporates are very careful in selecting people who have acquired good knowledge and produced good results in the industry rather than the number of years of experience you have spent. In the yesteryears, it was assumed that you could produce good results in an organization if you had sufficient number of years of experience. But there has been a paradigm shift in such assumption. Now it is more of what knowledge you have acquired rather than your several years of service. Experience definitely counts but experience with the same knowledge over several years without acquiring new knowledge and skills is not appreciated. This is called “Repeat Experience”, that is, experience with the same knowledge and skills over the years as against “New Experience” which is acquiring newer and newer knowledge and putting them to practice as you grow year by year. 
    13→
    14→**Grow by KNOWLEDGE, not by age**. But the question is how do you evaluate yourself on the quantum of knowledge you have achieved every year which has been put to practice? There is a simple method.
    15→
    16→Ask yourself and note down what things you know today which you did not know a year back. Also note on what are these new things that you have learnt which has been put to practice. It will be interesting to find out on your own. After you have noted down all the areas of knowledge and skills that you gathered during the past year, you need to also ponder on how important were they for an individual and the organization to grow up? If you have acquired knowledge on some trivial matters pertaining to new vendors, it is not so significant to you or to your organization’s growth. So you have to understand whether the new things that you have learnt are significant for the growth of the organization or not or is it that the new knowledge you gained is quite common with others in your organization or competitors. But if you have learnt proficiently on the process of hedging enabling you to participate in international tenders requiring fixed prices, then such knowledge is definitely significant. Today, amidst competition amongst your peers, you need to stand out in the crowd by acquiring impactful knowledge not common to others. Then only you may conclude that you have really grown by KNOWLEDGE and not by AGE and that you may have an edge over others to grow up the corporate ladder as well as grow your organization compared to your competitors.
    17→
    18→I want to stress on another important point. We know that ‘Knowledge is Power’. I differ slightly. In my opinion, ‘Knowledge is Power, if implemented’. There is no point in saying that someone is very knowledgeable. Well he may be so but it is meaningless to an organization until he implements that for the growth of the organization. I have brought out this point because I have seen seniors who were knowledgeable in one area of subject but ran out of practical experience to implement those in the organization. And that is why ‘experience’ and not necessarily ‘number of years of experience’ is important. That is what “New experience” brings on the table rather than “Repeat experience”. You may have knowledge on how to use a saw to cut a piece of wood, but you must have experience to use the saw and cut the wood. Similarly a surgeon might have studied on how to operate a particular organ in a human being, but he must have the hands on experience to use the scissors and other accessories and make use of the equipment and facilities to operate on the person. 
    19→
    20→Therefore, knowledge with the talent and experience is actually meaningful in an organization when implemented. I have found in many organisations that there were people on hot seats but with less knowledge and less “New experience”. They secured those positions only by the number of years of experience. However, that trend in today’s corporate is changing as I have illustrated above. 
    21→
    22→In fact, many organisations conducted ‘level determination’ assessment (you may call them interviews) for all its senior employees through a third party technical assessor to determine whether an individual is really worth the position he holds. Interestingly, the assessment proposed some demotions and some elevations for certain seniors. The organization may not have demoted those recommended demotion cases, but at least their promotions thereafter were stalled. And those with recommendations of elevation were promoted sooner or later in phases.
    23→
    24→While you work, try to acquire new relevant knowledge as much as possible which will have significant impact on the organization’s growth. This may be through regular interaction with seniors, attending various semesters, studying relevant books, pursuing relevant courses conducted by reputed industries or pursuing good online courses, etc. Knowledge will never go to waste. Many a times it has been seen that obtaining good degrees while at work may not give you immediate promotion, but the learning and experience you gather will someday boost your position up the ladder.  
    25→
    26→Grow by KNOWLEDGE, not by AGE is the mantra.
    27→
    28→**The salient points are:**
    29→
    30→1. Acquire impactful knowledge every year. Grow by knowledge, not by age  
    31→2. Number of years of experience is not significant today, what is significant is the level of knowledge that you have put to practice  
    32→3. Knowledge is Power, only if implemented. Knowledge not put to practice does not help you or the organization to grow.  
    33→4. Degrees or diplomas obtained while at work through part time or distance learning courses may not be so relevant to your organization for your promotion, but the knowledge you gain in these courses and put to practice to achieve better results may result in your promotion.  
    34→5. Even while at work, try to gain knowledge through various sources including joining part time or distance learning courses. Knowledge never goes to waste. Some time or later it will benefit you.
    35→

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : IMAGE-NISATION**
     9→
    10→I am not affiliated to any political party. Whichever party does well with respect to others, I vote for them. And I think that is the way it should be. I can always criticise the government I have not voted for, but that should be positive criticism which may lead to rectification by the government. But to criticise just for the sake of criticism because I am not a supporter of a government is anti-nationalism. Criticism must be for the development of the nation, not just only because I am sitting in the opposition bench. 
    11→
    12→Why I have stated a political scenario is that I find the same happening in many corporates. Often you may find that there are disloyal traitors working in your company. They earn a livelihood from the company, yet bad names it at every other opportunity. You have every right to criticize a company’s policy but you can do so in a positive manner by bringing it to the notice of your superiors so that the management can rectify the same as deemed fit. But you have no right to bad name the company’s image, particularly in the outside world. It not only spoils the image of the company, unknowingly it also spoils your own image and the image of all the employees in the company.
    13→
    14→We had a three day business unity meeting besides the Teesta river in West Bengal with river side camps for our stay. The entire program was conducted by a Pune consultant. After a daylong of team oriented games, we were in a bonfire in the late evening along with the consultant. Some of us administered courage to badmouth the company and point out some bad things prevalent with an expectation that the consultant will inform the Top management about the same. After many such complaints were lodged one after another by different employees, what the consultant said kept us quiet thereafter. The consultant said “You are here by your choice”. The company has not tied up your hands and legs so that you cannot cross over to another company of your choice. If you do not like a company, you better quit but do not bad mouth it for the sake of the company and other employees who are working for a livelihood. You have every right to place your grievance on the table, but badmouthing a company will do no good to anyone.
    15→
    16→So long one works in a company, you must create an image of the company within and in the outside world. An elevated image of a company where you work shall give you lot of satisfaction and pride in the company as well as in the society, neighborhood and amongst relatives and friends. Whenever I was asked by someone as to where I was working and I said “Tata ………….”, they said “Oh Tata\! Great\! They even did not listen to whether I was saying Tata Projects or Tata Power or Tata International. The name ‘Tata’ was itself enough to provide a high opinion to the outside world. This is what I call “Image-nisation”
    17→
    18→Once I went to a musical instrument market on a Saturday to purchase a synthesizer for myself. My budget was a maximum of Rs 8,000. After a lot of search in different shops, I finalized one model. But the price was Rs 12,000 for the model I chose. I was ready to pay but I had only Rs 8,000 in my pocket. So when I was leaving the shop, the shopkeeper asked “what happened?” I said I do not have that much money now. He asked me “where do I work?” I said I work in Tata ……… On hearing the name, the shopkeeper jumped in joy and gave me the instrument. He said ‘pay me your Rs 8,000 now and I will collect the balance Rs 4,000 from your office’. The guy came to my office the next Monday and collected Rs 4,000 from me. I just thought any other person may not have got the same deal, but the ‘Tata’ name did the wonders.
    19→
    20→Like above, there have been many instances in my life that I have been given preferences just because I worked in a reputed organization. “Image-nisation” creates a huge impact for the growth of an organization but it also facilitates one in many ways in his personal life. It gives a distinct position amongst your neighbours, relatives, friends and the society. 
    21→
    22→I want to give a disclaimer here. Above examples were from my own experience in the Tata group. It can happen with any other reputed organization which has a good name in the industry and in the outside world. It is all about the image of an organization, and that is what “Image-nisation” is.
    23→
    24→**The salient points are:**
    25→
    26→1. Create a good image of your organization as long as you work in a company. This is “Image-nisation”  
    27→2. Image-nisation distinctly uplifts your position in the society.  
    28→3. Image-nisation provides a feel good factor and an enthusiasm to work  
    29→4. Criticism can always be made, but in a positive manner to enable the company to amend as deemed fit  
    30→5. If you remain unhappy in your organization, have the guts to quit. Badmouthing is cowardice. It spoils the image of your other employees
    31→

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO HEED TO COST & QUALITY**
     9→
    10→Every penny you save for the company, you increase its profits. Everyone in their own jobs must find out ways to save money for the company. You can save money by implementing procurement policies, bulk purchase, alternate forms of transport, through financial terms, hiring time based consultants instead of permanent employees, design optimisation, etc. Even a driver can save fuel of his car by driving at optimum speeds. Everyone can contribute his bit in his job. But you need to be conscious. 
    11→
    12→I have come across cost unconscious people who are neither concerned on cost and when pointed out they say “what is the harm, the company is going to pay for it”. They fail to understand that aggregate of such escalated costs increases the overheads of the company and the company may then find it difficult to compete with its competitors. Big or small, people across all levels should try to decrease costs.
    13→
    14→However, I am not saying you need to save money even at the cost of quality and safety. Quality must be adhered to. However, it may not be necessary to have the best quality always. Reasonably good quality product or service suffices most of the times. You may not necessarily have a “Bose” speaker at home, it’s too costly, but a Philips or a Sony or any other comparable brand may be enough for your purpose. Like a Top executive may not always travel in a Business Class flight, rather he can take the Economy class or a first class train ticket which may be equally quite comfortable for his travel. In this way, he can cut costs. A designer must design optimally. A casual safe design will increase the cost of the structure and therefore the Marketing team will face difficulty to compete in the bids.
    15→
    16→Once a leader installed a very large TV in the company premises and displayed the progress of all running projects. The system cost lakhs of rupees which I feel was unnecessarily spent. Public displays are required when data changes or may change every minute, like the dashboard of a stock market platform, or the departures/arrivals in an airport or a big railway station, or the status of transport logistics of a big logistics company, etc. That means such public displays are very useful when the data is dynamic in nature. But in a project oriented company where progress is gradual over a period of time, such public display of data every day is not worthwhile. Rather weekly progress report could be sent to employees on a dashboard in their computer systems for the benefit of all the employees. Constant display of the same progress throughout the day is not relevant and employees find it boring over a period of time. This happens because the data on progress report of projects is basically static in nature. I found that after some days employees hardly looked on the TV since they already knew the progress from the weekly dashboards.
    17→
    18→In another instance, a hotel auditorium was booked for a program involving only few employees. This program could have been easily conducted in the conference room of the company. But there was another internal consultant delivering lecture in the conference room on that day. The HR Head should have been proactive to set different dates for the two programs in which case the program scheduled in the hotel auditorium could have taken place in the company’s conference room. This could have saved significant money for the company.
    19→
    20→Another example was a manager going to Lucknow for a meeting intentionally fixed up a meeting in Kanpur several days after so that he could fly twice to and fro and accumulate his flying miles on his credit card. Kanpur, being very close to Lucknow, the meeting in Kanpur could have been set subsequently after the meeting in Lucknow, thereby saving one flying trip for the company. But that was not to be so for vested interests of the individual.
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. Each individual in a company must strive to cut costs in all areas of work  
    25→2. Cost cuts are appreciated but not at the cost of sacrificing quality  
    26→3. Most of the times reasonably good quality of products or services suffice rather than the best quality products or services  
    27→4. Cost cutting helps a company to reduce overheads and be competitive  
    28→5. Cost cutting may help the company to pass on the benefits to its own employees

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE UNETHICAL MONEY**
     9→
    10→Unfortunately, dishonest people exist across all companies. Probably dishonesty is inherited from greed of money. It may have been cultivated in your mind while you were young depending on how, where and in which social environment you were brought up, how your elders and teachers have taught you, etc.  Most of dishonest cases happen in lieu of money. 
    11→
    12→An employee in Marketing or a Design Engineer can divulge secret information to a competitor in exchange of money. An employee in the Supply Chain Management or Administration or Safety or in IT or Technology or in Advertisement can procure equipment from a preferred buyer at a higher rate in exchange of favours.  An employee in Bidding may intentionally quote higher and lose a bid against its competitor for exchange of money from the competitor. A Project Manager can jack up a site subcontractor’s price intentionally for exchange of money. A HR employee can hire a consultant or a trainer at a higher Service fees in exchange of money. Finance or Accounts personnel can make some jugglery in their books of accounts for exchange of money. An Auditor may let go off a serious offence of a subcontractor for exchange of money. Insurance personnel can jack up the claim amount for exchange of money.
    13→
    14→So, you can observe that various people can be dishonest in so many ways for want of unethical money.
    15→
    16→I will only say that honest people must keep their eyes and ears open to notice such activities. The Vigilant Department should also be watchful. The Audit team can also do their bit. F\&A team can verify discrepancies. The HR should initiate a system whereby the employees can anonymously inform such activities in the forum. I said ‘anonymous’ because the employee may fear of being victimized later. The HR should verify such anonymous information and act strictly on them. Many say some anonymous representations could be false because of jealousy or anger against someone and hence all anonymous representations should be ignored. But such representations could also be true sometimes. Hence the top management has to be vigilant to examine all such cases and take actions appropriately. Airports too sometimes receive hoax calls or mails of terrorism, but since they could be life threatening all such calls are duly examined.     
    17→
    18→Once, a BU Head asked his site manager to provide quotations from three local subcontractors for executing specified works required in an ongoing project. The site manager in a week’s time provided three such quotations. The quotes were very competitive, say, Rs 78 Crores, Rs 75 Crores and Rs 80 Crores. So the subcontractor who quoted Rs 75 Crores was negotiated to Rs 74 Crores and subsequently an order was placed on them. When the execution started at site, some other subcontractors who were not asked to quote appealed to the company that they too should have been given a chance. On investigation, it was found that the three quotes given by the subcontractor were the same entity in different names. It was also found that had other subcontractors quoted, their quote would have been in the range of Rs 70 Crores. So the company lost Rs 4 Crores of money due to fraud conducted by the subcontractor with the knowledge of the site manager. The site manager had done this in exchange of money at the cost of his own company’s financials.
    19→
    20→One way to understand such fraud is to keep technical auditors who have had experience at site and as a Project Manager earlier in his life. Auditors with relevant technical experience in the past can only find out such frauds. The HR must also remove such site managers from the company which would give a stern signal to other dishonest employees. Promotions of suspected employees must be stalled.
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. Dishonest people exist across all companies  
    25→2. Dishonesty primarily occurs due to want of unethical money  
    26→3. The Vigilance team, Auditor and the HR team must detect dishonest employees with their intelligent vigilance and stern action taken against them  
    27→4. Employees must be assured that dishonesty has no place in the company

     8→**CHAPTER : DISRESPECT & UNGRATEFULNESS**

    10→I have seen sometimes that some juniors had more knowledge than their seniors and these seniors because of ego and jealousy treat their juniors with disrespect. Also I have observed that if a junior have performed very well and have been noticed by the leaders of his boss, and is acknowledged by those leaders for performing extraordinarily, the boss is annoyed for not getting the credit from his leaders. This happens due to jealousy and therefore leads to disrespecting his juniors. Ego and jealousy leading to disrespect is a very bad human trait.
    11→
    12→If a junior has better knowledge than you in a particular subject, it does not mean that you are not overall superior to him. You must have had better credentials because of which you are at a senior position. But in cases where a junior displays better wisdom, you must appreciate and pay respect to him and his knowledge. Leave your ego and jealousy behind. After all, his excellence in the subject would do well to your BU, not someone else’s.
    13→
    14→As a leader, you must show respect to all employees. You may punish employees for offences as per the company’s procedures, but even punishment can be meted out with respect. The above example demonstrates respect by top leaders even for a security staff. Respecting elderly and senior people is obvious, but respecting junior staff, either due to their knowledge, performance or actions also deserves respect.     17→
    18→Now, let us talk of gratefulness. If anyone, be it in your society or neighborhood or in the company you work for be a junior or a senior or a common man, you must be grateful if someone has done good to you or done good to your company or the society.     19→
    20→Remember Samson of Indian cricket team in the T20 World cup cricket final in 2026; he was discarded from the playing list earlier because of non-performance but was roped in by the coach and captain as a replacement of an injured player.     21→
    22→However, once I retired, I suddenly found them to be ignorant of me. They did not call me even to enquire about my health and well-being, leave alone for coming to my house for a chit chat. People who mattered to them were his immediate bosses, not me. I understood in life that unfortunately “so called” gratefulness is linked to your “desires and benefits”. Once you are not in a position to provide benefits to someone, you are no more useful to him and therefore people are ungrateful to you. It does not matter to them anymore for being ungrateful since you are “no one” in the company now.  I remember our respected Gaur Gopal Das, a young brilliant engineer in service but left his job to lead a life of a monk, now travelling worldwide to share his wisdom. Interestingly the “how” and “who” comprise of the same characters. It is only arranged differently when you become different without money or power.  
    23→
    24→Well, the question is what gratefulness gives to your life. It brings solace of mind, happiness and satisfaction by acknowledging the contribution someone had made for your erstwhile growth. Gratefulness is being divine. 
    25→
    26→You must have heard about “Tarpan” during Pitripoksh which many perform on the day of Mahalaya before commencement of the Durga Puja. It’s about paying respect and being grateful to your parents for all that they have done to you in your life. They may be no more but you should not forget that whatever you are today, you are because of them. 
    27→
    28→I also read one story about our President Abdul Kalam. He watched someone in office always deeply engrossed in his work. So one day he asked the officer to go home on time and take his children for a movie. Gladly the officer purchased the movie tickets in advance and was excited for the event. That day Sir Dr. Kalam again observed that the officer was deeply engrossed in his work. The officer later realised that time had gone away for the movie and sadly went back home with a terrible guilt on his face. When he said “sorry” to his wife, she asked “why”. He said I forgot to take my children to the movie. Then she said “our children have already watched the movie”. He wondered “how?” She said Sir Dr. Kalam had come to our residence and took our children to the movie. The officer was in shock. This is the level of gratitude even a senior most respected leader had shown to his junior officer.
    29→
    30→Be grateful to all who have contributed in your life in some way or the other. It does not harm you in any way. I still remember that I was always grateful to my boss for decades even after his retirement till his death because I always acknowledged that in whatever success I achieved as a top leader it was because of him. I made it a point that I should be visiting him at least once a year however busy I was in my corporate life. It was my personal KRA. And the joy and happiness we had chatting together every time is still etched in my mind. As you grow in your life, you will understand that money do bring happiness but such happiness does not last long, but relationships, gratefulness, contentment in life bring eternal happiness to human beings. 
    31→
    32→**The salient points are:**
    33→
    34→1. Respect all people around, juniors or seniors  
    35→2. Respecting people does not mean you cannot punish him for his misdeeds. Punishment too can be meted out respectfully  
    36→3. Respecting juniors for their exemplary achievements does not strip you away from your leadership  
    37→4. Be grateful to all people who have helped and contributed to your life, whether in your personal life or in your corporate life, even when they may not be in a position to help you further   
    38→5. Gratefulness is your own way of acknowledging your success
    39→

     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Create a good communication channel between people**  
    11→Leaders and HR should email and keep employees abreast of people oriented policies. Effective communication fosters transparency and trust amongst employees.
    12→
    13→**Create trust amongst people**  
    14→Treat people equally. Treat them with empathy in times of distress. Participate with them in times of joy. Tell them that you are an important member of the organization, irrespective of the position he holds.
    15→
    16→**Create capability**  
    17→Capability building of employees is a great contributor to an organization to remain afloat amidst stiff competition. Training programs in different fields must be organized from time to time. 
    18→
    19→**Create time consciousness**  
    20→In our life, we can get back money lost before, we can get back friends missed before, we can get back good life if missed before, and we can probably get back almost everything that was lost before, EXCEPT the time you lost. There is a saying “Time and tide waits for none”. Even the wealthiest on earth cannot get back time spent or lost. So treat time as one of your biggest asset in life. We live our life only once, hence time is too precious for us. Utilise your time most effectively so that you can grow faster and so does the organization.
    21→
    22→**Create an environment of bonding**  
    23→Bonding between people in an organization is of significant importance. They will fight for the organization in times of difficulty and distress and also help each other in their personal lives. 
    24→
    25→**Avoid oversharing achievements**  
    26→Do not repeatedly emphasise achievement of a particular individual. It will break the morale of other team members who will think as if they have not contributed anything.
    27→
    28→**Adhere to policies**  
    29→Maintain proper dress code and follow company rules. These will create a good image and culture of the organization.

After a daylong of team-oriented games, we were in a bonfire in the late evening along with the consultant. "The company has not tied up your hands and legs so that you cannot cross over to another company of your choice. 1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - People, the Employees"
     3→part: 2
     4→section: 2

     6→---
     7→
     8→**PEOPLE, THE EMPLOYEES**
     9→
    10→**The role of people in a corporate**  
    11→As we know and say, people are the most important assets of an organisation. An organisation does not comprise merely a name and a huge building made of bricks and mortar. It’s the people who make the name and fame of an organisation; it’s the people who execute the business to achieve its objectives and build reputation for the organisation. 
    12→
    13→Other assets are also important. But unlike the yesteryears, they are comparatively easier to build. For example, an organisation today, even if its size is small, has multiple sources to obtain finance, be it banks or other financial institutions, at low interest rates. With such finance, it can purchase good quality equipment and other infrastructure, software and hardware to conduct its business. With money, you can easily invest in good quality assets for your business.
    14→
    15→But what about good quality professionals? Unfortunately, they don’t come with an ISO stamp as a product or service does. Yes, they do come with academic degrees behind them, but it has been proven time and again that the best academic professionals of reputed universities or colleges are not necessarily the best experienced and talented people to deliver in organisations. Academic degrees do provide the necessary criteria to appear for an interview and are indicative of supposedly good performance, but the HR Head has to be very cautious to select a professional who can practically deliver the best in the industry.
    16→
    17→Choosing the right candidate today is difficult, more so in a populous country with millions having the required academic degrees and experience. But today you need to choose the best and leave out even the good ones. Here is why it’s so.
    18→
    19→For example, in a tender driven business, you need to become L1 with a technical and a financial responsive bid. Even if you have submitted a very good bid but are L2 or higher by a thin margin, it has no meaning except for condolences from your boss for good work. Because the L1 presumably wins the contract and once you execute the contract, not only the proceeds contribute to the finances of the organisation, but also enable the organisation to realise the profits and pay your salaries, increment, bonus, bear the overheads and grow the organisation. The L2 bidder has no significance except for a consolation that its price was competitive. Therefore you need the best people in the industry, to become L1 as in the above example.
    20→
    21→Today bad people are flocking all around. Good people are abundant but the best are scarce. So the HR has a tough job to find the best which is required to keep the organisation afloat amidst stiff competition and continuous changing environment with each passage of time.
    22→
    23→Also it may be noted that organisations who have long-term plans in a particular business domain need to recruit the best though their salary demand may be higher. Investment in the best of people will result in good financials in the long run. In Bengali, there is a well-known phrase. It goes like this: “Sastar teen obostha” which means “buying anything cheap will lead to three disasters”. 
    24→
    25→It is the best people in the industry who carve a niche for their organisation and create such products and services which eventually become their USP. For example, look at the GE engines used by aircraft manufacturers. They have almost become indispensable. It is GE’s USP. 
    26→
    27→Hire the best, use the best and be the best in the industry. If you recruit average people, don’t expect anything better than average business results.
    28→
